title: Jakarta EE (JEE) Course – Quiz Questions v3
description: Multiple-choice questions covering the full JEE course material, ordered by course chapter. Code-based questions are marked with 'code': true. Approximately 1 code question per 4 conceptual questions.

questions.question: Jakarta EE was created primarily to help developers build which type of application?
options: A) Robust, secure, scalable distributed enterprise applications
B) Desktop GUI applications using Swing, with platform-independent rendering via the Java2D graphics pipeline
C) Static websites served over FTP, with HTML files stored directly on the server filesystem for download
D) Mobile apps for Android and iOS using a shared Java codebase compiled down to native platform bytecode
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE was created to facilitate the development of robust, highly available, secure, and scalable distributed applications.

questions.question: In September 2019, Java EE was transferred to which foundation and renamed?
options: A) Oracle retained it — renamed Oracle EE, continuing closed governance under Oracle's commercial stewardship
B) Linux Foundation — renamed OpenEE, hosting the project under the same umbrella as the Linux kernel spec
C) Apache Foundation — renamed Apache EE, governed under the Apache Software License version 2.0 terms
D) Eclipse Foundation — renamed Jakarta EE
questions.explanation: In September 2019, Java EE became open source with the new name Jakarta EE, with its standards transferred to the Eclipse Open Source Foundation.

questions.question: A Jakarta EE Profile is best described as:
options: A) A user account stored in the admin console, used to log in and configure the running application server
B) A smaller, targeted subset of the full Jakarta EE platform
C) An XML logging configuration file that sets log levels, handlers, and appenders for the server runtime
D) A database connection pool definition that specifies the JNDI name, driver class, and pool size limits
questions.explanation: Profiles are subsets of the platform introduced to enable adherence to standards on smaller perimeters, such as the Web Profile or Core Profile.

questions.question: Which Jakarta EE profile contains the fewest specifications and targets microservices runtimes?
options: A) Core Profile
B) Web Profile — medium subset
C) Full Platform — all specifications
D) Micro Profile — cloud-native only
questions.explanation: The Core Profile has even fewer specifications than the Web Profile and is designed for microservices and early (ahead-of-time) compilation.

questions.question: 'Convention over Configuration' in Jakarta EE means developers must:
options: A) Only specify configuration that differs from the defaults
B) Configure every bean explicitly in beans.xml before the CDI container will recognise or inject it at all
C) Avoid annotations and rely on XML deployment descriptors instead to configure every application behavior
D) Always write explicit XML configuration for every default behavior, even standard well-known conventions
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE uses Convention over Configuration — there is no need to define the most common behaviors by metadata. Only exceptions to the defaults need explicit configuration.

questions.question: Which of the following is one of the three main Jakarta EE container types?
options: A) SQL Container
B) Python Container
C) CDI Container
D) Docker Container
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE defines three main container types: the EJB Container, the Web (Servlet) Container, and the CDI Container.

questions.question: What is the primary job of a Jakarta EE container?
options: A) Provide services (security, transactions, lifecycle) to components
B) Render HTML pages to the end user in the browser by interpreting JSF Facelets templates server-side
C) Compile Java source code to JVM bytecode, performing incremental builds and annotation processing steps
D) Store and retrieve data from relational databases by directly managing the underlying JDBC connection pool
questions.explanation: Containers provide critical services and features, allowing developers to focus on implementing business logic rather than solving low-level technical problems.

questions.question: Which archive format bundles multiple modules (WAR + JAR) into one deployable unit?
options: A) .zip
B) .ear
C) .war
D) .jar
questions.explanation: An EAR (Enterprise Archive) bundles multiple modules — web modules (.war) and EJB modules (.jar) — into a single deployable artifact.

questions.question: Jakarta EE supports two ways to supply configuration metadata. What are they?
options: A) YAML files and JSON files placed inside the META-INF configuration directory of the application archive
B) Environment variables and .properties files only
C) Annotations and XML deployment descriptors
D) Java source code comments parsed at build time and README files loaded via ClassLoader resource streams
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE uses a declarative programming mode via annotations (attached to code) and deployment descriptors (XML configuration files).

questions.question: In the Jakarta EE three-tier architecture, which tier contains the Web Browser and App Client?
options: A) Client Tier
B) Business Logic Tier
C) Data/Resource Tier
D) Web/Presentation Tier
questions.explanation: The Client Tier contains the Web Browser (HTML/JS) or App Client (Swing/CLI) that communicates with the server via HTTP or REST.

questions.question: Which of the following is an open-source full Jakarta EE application server?
options: A) Node.js — a JavaScript server runtime, not a Jakarta EE application server implementation
B) GlassFish
C) Nginx — a high-performance HTTP proxy and web server, not a Jakarta EE application server
D) Apache Tomcat
questions.explanation: GlassFish is an open-source Jakarta EE application server. Tomcat implements only the Servlet/JSP portion, not the full specification.

questions.question: What major addition did Jakarta EE 10 introduce?
options: A) The Core Profile for microservices and lean runtimes
B) Virtual thread support via Project Loom integration, enabling lightweight concurrency in all servlet code
C) Jakarta Data 1.0 as its primary new API, unifying repository-style data access across persistence providers
D) Full SOAP web service support via a reimplemented JAX-WS 4.0 specification with WSDL 2.0 compliance
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE 10 (September 2022) introduced the Core Profile — a minimal profile designed for microservices and ahead-of-time compilation runtimes.

questions.question:
Look at this Maven pom.xml snippet. What does this dependency declaration do?

<dependency>
  <groupId>jakarta.platform</groupId>
  <artifactId>jakarta.jakartaee-api</artifactId>
  <version>10.0.0</version>
  <scope>provided</scope>
</dependency>
options: A) It restricts the project to using only Jakarta EE Web Profile APIs, excluding full-platform dependencies entirely
B) It makes the full Jakarta EE 10 API available at compile time but expects the application server to provide it at runtime
C) It bundles the full Jakarta EE API JAR inside the deployed .war file so the API travels with the application
D) It downloads the Jakarta EE 10 runtime binaries and installs them automatically as the application server
questions.explanation: The 'provided' scope means the Jakarta EE API jar is used during compilation but will NOT be bundled in the artifact — the application server supplies it at runtime.

questions.question:
A developer builds a web application and packages it. What type of archive is produced, and where does it get deployed?

$ mvn package
# produces: target/myapp.war
# then deployed to Payara Server
options: A) A .ear file — required for all Jakarta EE web deployments, bundling web modules and EJB modules together
B) A .zip file — extracted by the application server at startup using a proprietary expansion protocol handler
C) A .war (Web Application Archive) — deployed to the Web Container of the application server, making servlets and REST endpoints accessible
D) A .jar file — deployed to the EJB container only and exposing remote business interfaces via RMI-IIOP
questions.explanation: mvn package produces a .war file for a web application. The .war is deployed to the Web Container (e.g., Payara, WildFly), which hosts servlets, JAX-RS resources, JSF pages, and WebSocket endpoints.

questions.question:
Looking at the Jakarta EE Application Architecture, a request comes in from a Web Browser. What is the correct path through the tiers for a typical REST call that reads data from a database?

Web Browser → ??? → ??? → ??? → Database
options: A) Web Browser → EJB Container (handles the HTTP request via remote EJB) → Web Container (JAX-RS) → Persistence Layer → Database
B) Web Browser → Web Container (JAX-RS) → Business Layer (CDI/EJB) → Persistence Layer (JPA) → Database
C) Web Browser → Database (direct JDBC connection) → Persistence Layer (ORM cache) → Web Container → Browser
D) Web Browser → Persistence Layer (direct ORM call) → Web Container (JSF view) → EJB Container → Database
questions.explanation: A typical REST request flows: Web Browser → Web Container (where JAX-RS resource handles HTTP) → Business Layer (CDI beans or EJBs with business logic) → Persistence Layer (JPA/EntityManager) → Database.

questions.question: What is Apache Maven's primary role in a Java project?
options: A) Running integration tests only, with no support for compilation, packaging, or dependency resolution tasks
B) Providing a graphical debugger that attaches to a running JVM and visualises heap memory and thread states
C) Monitoring application performance at runtime by collecting JMX metrics and exposing a live web dashboard
D) Automating builds, dependency management, and the project lifecycle
questions.explanation: Maven facilitates and automates both the management and construction of a Java project, including dependency management, compilation, testing, and packaging.

questions.question: The file that describes a Maven project's structure and dependencies is called:
options: A) pom.xml
B) maven-settings.xml
C) project.xml
D) build.xml
questions.explanation: Maven uses pom.xml (Project Object Model) to describe the project, its dependencies, plugins, and build configuration.

questions.question: A dependency with Maven scope 'provided' will:
options: A) Be downloaded only when running unit tests — excluded from production builds and the final deployed artifact
B) Be fetched exclusively from a private Nexus or Artifactory repository as configured in settings.xml files
C) Be bundled inside the final .war archive, overriding any version already provided by the application server
D) Be available at compile time; the app server provides it at runtime — not packaged
questions.explanation: The 'provided' scope means the application server supplies the API at runtime; Maven includes it for compilation but excludes it from the packaged artifact.

questions.question: Running 'mvn package' on a Jakarta EE project produces what file type for deployment?
options: A) An .xml descriptor file containing compiled structural metadata about the project dependencies and modules
B) A .war or .jar file ready for deployment
C) A .tar.gz archive containing the compiled bytecode classes together with all transitive dependency sources
D) A single .class file only — compilation outputs one bytecode file representing the main application entry point
questions.explanation: The 'package' lifecycle phase compiles, tests, and packages the project into a .war (web project) or .jar (library/EJB module).

questions.question: Maven's Central Repository is:
options: A) The ~/.m2/repository folder on your local machine where Maven caches all previously downloaded artifacts
B) The default remote store Maven downloads missing artifacts from
C) The application server's deployment directory where packaged .war and .ear deployment files are placed
D) A Git repository hosted by the Apache Software Foundation containing Maven plugin and archetype source code
questions.explanation: The Central Repository is the default public remote repository Maven uses to download dependencies not found in the local repository.

questions.question: Which is the correct order of Maven's default lifecycle phases?
options: A) package → compile → test → validate → install
B) validate → compile → test → package → install
C) test → compile → validate → install → package
D) compile → validate → test → package → install
questions.explanation: Maven's default lifecycle runs: validate, compile, test, package, install (then deploy). Each phase depends on previous phases completing.

questions.question: What does Maven's local repository store?
options: A) Only the source code of your current project under the src/ tree, not any third-party dependency JARs
B) Plugin configuration XML fragments shared globally across every Maven project on the developer machine
C) Compiled bytecode of the running application server, including all container-provided and runtime class files
D) All downloaded and locally-installed dependency artifacts (JARs, POMs)
questions.explanation: Maven's local repository (~/.m2/repository) caches all downloaded dependencies and locally installed artifacts, avoiding repeated downloads.

questions.question:
You want to use the Jakarta EE Web Profile in your project instead of the full platform. Which artifactId should you use in pom.xml?

<dependency>
  <groupId>jakarta.platform</groupId>
  <artifactId>???</artifactId>
  <version>10.0.0</version>
  <scope>provided</scope>
</dependency>
options: A) jakarta.jakartaee-web-api
B) jakarta.jakartaee-full-api
C) jakarta.jakartaee-core-api
D) jakarta.jakartaee-api — the full Jakarta EE platform artifact, including all specifications together
questions.explanation: jakarta.jakartaee-web-api is used for the Web Profile. jakarta.jakartaee-api is for the full platform, and jakarta.jakartaee-core-api is for the Core Profile.

questions.question:
You run 'mvn package' on a Jakarta EE project. Which of the following sequences of lifecycle phases does Maven actually execute?

$ mvn package
options: A) package → install → deploy — Maven runs the named phase and all subsequent lifecycle phases after it
B) compile → package only, silently skipping validate and test to give faster execution in CI environments
C) Only the 'package' phase in isolation — Maven executes exactly and only the single phase you specified
D) validate → compile → test → package — Maven runs all phases up to and including the one named
questions.explanation: Maven's lifecycle is sequential — running 'mvn package' executes all phases from the beginning up to and including 'package': validate, compile, test, then package.

questions.question: What is a Java annotation?
options: A) A block comment placed before a class or method that the Javadoc tool converts into published HTML documentation
B) A loop construct introduced in Java 5 for iterating cleanly over arrays and any Iterable collection type
C) Metadata attached to code elements, readable by the compiler or at runtime
D) A class that subclasses java.lang.Object with additional utility methods added via compile-time bytecode weaving
questions.explanation: Annotations provide metadata about code and can inform the compiler, be processed at build/deployment time, or be read via reflection at runtime.

questions.question: A developer types 'toSttring()' instead of overriding 'toString()'. Which annotation would have caught this mistake at compile time?
options: A) @Override
B) @FunctionalInterface
C) @Deprecated
D) @SuppressWarnings
questions.explanation: @Override tells the compiler the method must override a superclass method. A typo means no match is found, causing a compile error immediately.

questions.question: Which @Retention policy makes an annotation readable via reflection at runtime?
options: A) RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME
B) RetentionPolicy.BYTECODE
C) RetentionPolicy.CLASS — retained in the .class bytecode file but stripped by the JVM at class-loading time
D) RetentionPolicy.SOURCE — discarded by the compiler before .class file emission; absent from bytecode and runtime
questions.explanation: RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME retains the annotation in the bytecode and makes it accessible via reflection at runtime.

questions.question: The @Target meta-annotation controls:
options: A) Whether subclasses automatically inherit the annotation, which is actually controlled by the @Inherited meta-annotation
B) The relative processing order applied when multiple annotations of the same type are stacked on one element
C) The minimum Java language version required for the annotation to compile and be retained in class bytecode
D) Which code elements (TYPE, METHOD, FIELD, etc.) the annotation may be placed on
questions.explanation: @Target takes one or more ElementType values, restricting where the custom annotation can legally be applied.

questions.question: Which keyword declares a custom annotation type in Java?
options: A) annotation
B) @annotation
C) @class — a syntax that has never been valid in any version of the official Java language specification
D) @interface
questions.explanation: Custom annotations are declared with the @interface keyword, e.g., public @interface MyAnnotation { }.

questions.question: What effect does @Deprecated have when applied to a method?
options: A) It removes the method body entirely at compile time, replacing it with an UnsupportedOperationException throw
B) It prevents the method from being overridden in any subclass by implicitly adding a synthetic final modifier
C) It marks the method as obsolete and causes the compiler to warn callers
D) It causes the method to execute asynchronously on a managed executor thread pool, returning a Future result
questions.explanation: @Deprecated marks a method (or class/field) as obsolete. The compiler issues a warning whenever deprecated code is called.

options: A) The 'name' element has no default value, so it must be provided — @MyAnnotation(quantity=2) is missing a value for 'name'
B) Nothing is wrong — this is valid Java; annotation elements that have declared default values are always optional
C) You cannot apply annotations to classes in Java; annotations are only legal on method and field declarations
D) The 'default' keyword is not permitted inside @interface declaration bodies, causing a compilation failure
questions.explanation: Since 'name' has no default value, it is required. The usage @MyAnnotation(quantity=2) will fail to compile because 'name' is not provided. It should be @MyAnnotation(name="something", quantity=2).

options: A) Option A — correct: @interface with @Target(METHOD) and @Retention(RUNTIME) makes it available at runtime on methods
B) Option D — the curly-brace inline syntax shown is not valid Java annotation declaration syntax whatsoever
C) Option C — annotations are interfaces internally; a class cannot extend the java.lang.annotation.Annotation type
D) Option B — @Retention(SOURCE) causes the compiler to discard the annotation entirely, making it unavailable for reflection at runtime
questions.explanation: Option A is correct: @interface declares the annotation type, @Target(ElementType.METHOD) restricts it to methods, and @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) ensures it is available for reflection at runtime.

questions.question: A Jakarta Servlet is best described as:
options: A) A Java object handling HTTP requests in the Web Container
B) An XML deployment descriptor file configured inside WEB-INF that defines servlet-to-URL path mappings
C) A JNDI-registered connection pool manager that allocates and manages database connections for the application
D) A browser-side JavaScript UI component that runs inside the client DOM and communicates via the fetch() API
questions.explanation: A servlet is a Java object running in a Web Container, responding to HTTP requests and dynamically returning content.

questions.question: What is the recommended way to map a servlet class to a URL?
options: A) Call ServletContext.addServlet() at runtime
B) Name the class with a 'Servlet' suffix
C) Declare it in web.xml only, using explicit <servlet> and <servlet-mapping> elements for each URL pattern
D) Annotate the class with @WebServlet
questions.explanation: The @WebServlet annotation is the recommended, declarative way to register a servlet and map it to URL patterns without touching web.xml.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly lists the servlet lifecycle stages?
options: A) Loading → Instantiation → Initialization → Service → Destruction
B) Initialization → Loading → Service → Instantiation → Destruction
C) Service → Initialization → Loading → Destruction — processing requests before the servlet is even set up
D) Instantiation → Service → Loading → Initialization → Destruction
questions.explanation: The Web Container manages: loading the class, instantiating it, calling init(), then service() per request, and finally destroy().

questions.question: Which method handles HTTP GET requests in an HttpServlet?
options: A) handleGet()
B) service()
C) doGet()
D) doPost()
questions.explanation: Override doGet() to handle HTTP GET requests. HttpServlet.service() dispatches to doGet(), doPost(), etc. based on the HTTP verb.

questions.question: What does a Servlet Filter do?
options: A) Manages the JPA persistence context lifecycle per HTTP request, opening and closing EntityManager automatically
B) Renders HTML output from a Facelets XHTML template by evaluating EL expressions entirely on the server side
C) Intercepts requests/responses to add cross-cutting logic (auth, logging, encryption)
D) Sanitises all SQL query strings before they reach the JDBC driver to prevent SQL injection vulnerabilities
questions.explanation: Filters implement jakarta.servlet.Filter and sit in the request/response pipeline, enabling cross-cutting concerns like authentication, logging, and compression.

questions.question: Which scope stores attributes visible to ALL users across the entire application?
options: A) HttpSession — stores attributes visible only within one user's individual session across multiple requests
B) ServletContext
C) ServletRequest
D) ThreadLocal — stores data scoped only to the current executing thread, invisible across user sessions
questions.explanation: ServletContext is the application scope — attributes set on it are shared across all servlets, sessions, and requests in the application.

questions.question: The main benefit of asynchronous servlet processing is:
options: A) Merges multiple HTTP responses into a single batched reply to the client, reducing overall round-trip count
B) Causes the browser to parse and render HTML pages faster by enabling server-push via HTTP/2 push promises
C) Automatically caches the servlet response in a managed store, replaying it verbatim for identical requests
D) Frees the container thread while long work runs elsewhere, boosting scalability
questions.explanation: Asynchronous processing frees the container thread to accept new requests while a slow task runs elsewhere, improving overall server scalability.

questions.question: A Servlet Filter must implement which interface?
options: A) jakarta.servlet.HttpFilter
B) jakarta.servlet.Filter
C) jakarta.servlet.RequestInterceptor
D) jakarta.servlet.FilterChain
questions.explanation: Filters must implement jakarta.servlet.Filter, which defines the doFilter(request, response, chain) method where interception logic goes.

options: A) The server throws a NullPointerException because HttpServletRequest is null
B) The server returns a plain-text response body containing 'Hello!'
C) The server returns an HTTP 404 Not Found because no matching web.xml mapping exists alongside @WebServlet
D) The server returns a complete HTML document wrapping 'Hello!' in a generated head-and-body page structure
questions.explanation: The @WebServlet annotation maps the servlet to /hello. doGet() sets the content type to text/plain and writes 'Hello!' as the response body — the browser receives plain text.

options: A) Option C — application scope (ServletContext) shares data across all users and requests
B) Option B — session scope stores data per individual user session, so different users each get their own instance
C) Option A — request scope stores data for the current single request only and is discarded after the response
D) Option D — HTTP response headers are used to transmit state to the browser client, not to share server-side data
questions.explanation: ServletContext is the application-scoped object. Attributes set on it are visible to all servlets and all users. Request scope lasts only one request; session scope is per-user.

questions.question: Dependency Injection is an implementation of which design principle?
options: A) Single Responsibility Principle
B) Inversion of Control
C) Liskov Substitution Principle
D) Open/Closed Principle
questions.explanation: DI is one implementation technique of Inversion of Control — instead of a class creating its own dependencies, the container provides them.

questions.question: Which annotation injects a CDI bean into a field?
options: A) @Resource
B) @EJB — injects a remote or local EJB session bean reference, not a general CDI managed bean
C) @Inject
D) @Autowired
questions.explanation: @Inject is the standard CDI annotation for injecting a managed bean into a field, constructor, or initializer method.

questions.question: Which CDI scope creates one shared instance for the entire lifetime of the application?
options: A) @RequestScoped — creates a new instance per HTTP request, destroyed when the request completes
B) @SessionScoped — ties the bean lifecycle to the user's HTTP session, not the whole application
C) @ApplicationScoped
D) @TransactionScoped
questions.explanation: @ApplicationScoped creates a single bean instance that lives as long as the application, shared by all users and all requests.

questions.question: A shopping cart bean that must survive across multiple requests from the same user should use which scope?
options: A) @RequestScoped
B) @ApplicationScoped
C) @Dependent — creates a new instance per injection point, tied to the hosting bean lifecycle
D) @SessionScoped
questions.explanation: @SessionScoped ties the bean's lifecycle to the HTTP session — perfect for per-user state like a shopping cart that spans multiple page requests.

questions.question: When two beans implement the same interface, how does CDI know which one to inject?
options: A) It injects all matching beans simultaneously, wrapping them in an Instance<GreetingService> iterable collection
B) It throws an AmbiguousResolutionException at deployment time because only one implementation per type is permitted
C) It uses a shared Qualifier annotation on the injection point and the chosen bean
D) It always selects the bean declared first in the beans.xml discovery order when multiple candidates are present
questions.explanation: Qualifiers are custom annotations that disambiguate between multiple implementations. The injection point and the target bean share the same qualifier.

questions.question: After injecting a CDI bean, which lifecycle method is called immediately?
options: A) @PostConstruct
B) init() — a plain method name with no annotation, not automatically called by the CDI container lifecycle
C) @PreDestroy — called just before the container destroys the bean, not immediately after construction
D) onCreate() — a non-existent method name not defined anywhere in the Jakarta EE CDI specification
questions.explanation: After the container creates the bean and injects its dependencies, it calls the method annotated with @PostConstruct for any initialization logic.

questions.question: @Transactional in CDI acts as:
options: A) An enabler for asynchronous method execution, scheduling the call to run on a managed executor thread pool
B) A marker that turns the annotated class into a JPA @Entity persistable to a relational database table
C) A trigger that schedules the annotated method to run at fixed time intervals using a container timer service
D) An interceptor managing transaction begin/commit/rollback around annotated methods
questions.explanation: @Transactional is a CDI interceptor binding that wraps method calls with JTA transaction management.

questions.question: CDI Events decouple beans because:
options: A) The emitter and observer share only the event payload type — no direct dependency
B) Both beans must reside in the same Java package for the CDI event bus to resolve and deliver subscriptions
C) All CDI events are always dispatched asynchronously on a background thread pool managed by the container
D) CDI events are routed over JMS topics managed by the messaging provider, requiring a broker deployment
questions.explanation: CDI Events implement the Observer pattern: the emitter fires via Event.fire(); any observer annotated with @Observes on the matching type is called — without any direct reference.

questions.question: If beans.xml is absent from a Jakarta EE application, CDI will:
options: A) Automatically discover beans by scanning for CDI annotations
B) Disables all CDI features entirely — without beans.xml the container performs no injection of any kind
C) Requires every CDI-managed bean class to be listed explicitly as an entry inside the web.xml descriptor
D) Throws a deployment error indicating that mandatory CDI configuration is missing from the application archive
questions.explanation: beans.xml is optional. Without it, CDI performs implicit bean discovery by scanning classes annotated with CDI annotations.

questions.question: A CDI bean with no explicit scope annotation defaults to:
options: A) @RequestScoped — a new bean instance is created and destroyed for each individual incoming HTTP request
B) @Dependent — a new instance per injection point, lives as long as the host object
C) @SessionScoped — one shared bean instance is maintained per authenticated user's HTTP session lifetime
D) @ApplicationScoped — a single bean instance is created once and shared across the entire running application
questions.explanation: Beans without a scope annotation are @Dependent: a new instance is created per injection point and destroyed when the containing object is.

questions.question: A CDI Interceptor is used to:
options: A) Intercepts JMS messages arriving on a topic before they reach an MDB, applying filtering and routing logic
B) Adds cross-cutting behavior (transactions, auditing) to bean methods declaratively
C) Caches database query results transparently for annotated bean methods using a second-level cache store
D) Blocks unauthorised HTTP requests at the servlet filter layer by inspecting Authorization request headers
questions.explanation: CDI Interceptors wrap managed bean method invocations, allowing transversal concerns like transaction management, security, and logging to be added declaratively.

questions.question: A @Produces method in CDI is used when:
options: A) The bean needs to fire CDI events to other observer beans that are registered in the application context
B) A JMS TextMessage needs to be produced, serialised as a String payload, and dispatched to a queue destination
C) An EJB needs to declare and expose a remote business interface for cross-JVM client method invocations
D) Normal bean instantiation is not enough — a factory method controls object creation
questions.explanation: Producer methods act as @Produces factories for bean instances — useful when creating third-party class instances or when custom instantiation logic is required.

options: A) Dependent scope — the bean is created at startup, kept alive indefinitely, and is never garbage collected
B) Application scope — a single shared HelloWorldBean instance is used concurrently by all user requests
C) Session scope — the same HelloWorldBean instance is reused for all requests from one authenticated user
D) Request scope — a new HelloWorldBean instance is created and injected for each individual HTTP request
questions.explanation: @RequestScoped means a new instance of HelloWorldBean is created for each HTTP request and destroyed when the request completes. This matches the course diagram exactly.

options: A) doSomething() in ComplicatedService is recursively called again each time TaskCompleted fires the event
B) Nothing happens at runtime unless Logger is explicitly injected as a dependency into ComplicatedService
C) onTaskCompleted() in Logger is called because it is annotated with @Observes TaskCompleted
D) The CDI container automatically invokes every public method in Logger whenever any application event fires
questions.explanation: When event.fire() is called with a TaskCompleted instance, the CDI container finds all methods annotated with @Observes TaskCompleted and invokes them synchronously — here that is Logger.onTaskCompleted().

options: A) CDI resolves the ambiguity by selecting the bean whose class name sorts first alphabetically at startup
B) CDI uses the @Superior qualifier to select FormalGreeting specifically, because the injection point and the bean share the same qualifier
C) CDI throws an AmbiguousResolutionException at deployment because both beans implement GreetingService
D) @ApplicationScoped on both beans causes a scope conflict — only one application-scoped bean per interface is allowed
questions.explanation: CDI qualifiers resolve ambiguity. The @Inject @Superior injection point tells CDI to select the bean also annotated with @Superior — FormalGreeting. Without the qualifier, CDI would throw an unsatisfied or ambiguous resolution error.

questions.question: Which EJB type maintains no state between method calls and is pooled by the container?
options: A) Message-Driven Bean — triggered by JMS messages, not pooled for synchronous method invocations
B) Stateless Session Bean
C) Stateful Session Bean — retains conversational state per client across multiple method invocations
D) Singleton Session Bean
questions.explanation: Stateless Session Beans carry no per-client state. The container pools them and assigns one to handle each incoming request.

questions.question: Which EJB type is triggered by a JMS message arriving in a queue or topic?
options: A) Singleton EJB — exactly one instance per application, not activated by JMS queue messages
B) Stateful Session Bean — maintains per-client conversational state across multiple method calls
C) Message-Driven Bean (MDB)
D) Stateless Session Bean — pooled with no per-client state, not wired to a JMS destination
questions.explanation: Message-Driven Beans are activated by the container when a JMS message arrives, enabling asynchronous message consumption.

questions.question: How many instances of a @Singleton EJB exist per application?
options: A) As many instances as the container pool size allows, dynamically scaled up based on concurrent request load
B) One new instance per active HTTP request, created and destroyed alongside the Web Container request lifecycle
C) Exactly one, shared across the entire application
D) One instance per authenticated user HTTP session, similar in behaviour to a @SessionScoped CDI bean
questions.explanation: A Singleton EJB has exactly one instance per application, making it suitable for shared state or application-level caching.

questions.question: With Container Managed Transactions (CMT), who writes the begin/commit/rollback code?
options: A) The database trigger mechanism, firing stored procedures that implicitly wrap every DML statement in a transaction
B) Nobody — the container handles it declaratively
C) The CDI container via @Transactional exclusively
D) The developer, by explicitly calling UserTransaction.begin(), commit(), and rollback() in application code
questions.explanation: With CMT, the container handles transaction demarcation declaratively based on annotations or XML. No manual transaction code is required.

questions.question: All Jakarta Enterprise Beans are automatically also:
options: A) JAX-RS resources
B) JPA entities
C) Jakarta Servlets
D) CDI beans
questions.explanation: All EJBs are automatically CDI beans, benefiting from CDI features like injection, interceptors, and events.

questions.question: Which EJB type is best suited for a multi-step checkout process where server-side state must survive between user actions?
options: A) JAX-RS REST resources — EJBs are not automatically JAX-RS endpoints unless explicitly annotated with @Path
B) Stateful Session Bean
C) Stateless Session Bean
D) Singleton Session Beans only — other EJB types are not automatically treated as CDI beans by the container
questions.explanation: Stateful Session Beans maintain conversational state with a specific client across multiple calls — ideal for checkout wizards or shopping carts.

questions.question: @Remote vs @Local on an EJB interface: what is the difference?
options: A) @Remote uses RESTful HTTP transport; @Local uses SOAP/XML over an in-process serialised object protocol
B) @Remote enforces container-managed security checks; @Local bypasses all security for trusted same-JVM callers
C) @Remote accesses a remote relational database via distributed JDBC; @Local uses a local embedded database
D) @Remote allows calls from a different JVM; @Local is optimised for same-JVM calls
questions.explanation: @Remote marks the interface as callable from outside the JVM via RMI. @Local is for efficient in-process calls within the same application server.

options: A) A Message-Driven Bean; @LocalBean exposes a local listener interface enabling it to receive JMS messages directly
B) A Stateful Session Bean; @LocalBean signals that a separate business interface class must be declared for it
C) A Singleton EJB; @LocalBean marks it as the globally shared single application-level instance of this type
D) A Stateless Session Bean; @LocalBean means no separate business interface is needed — the class itself is the interface for local use
questions.explanation: @Stateless marks it as a Stateless Session Bean. @LocalBean means there is no need to declare a separate business interface — the bean class itself can be used directly for local (same-JVM) calls.

questions.question:
What type of EJB should you use for this use case, and which annotation declares it?

You need to implement a scheduled job that cleans up expired sessions in the database every hour.

Hint: Timer EJBs use @Schedule.
options: A) @MessageDriven — MDBs can be triggered by a timer-backed topic using a JMS message selector configuration
B) @Singleton with @Schedule — a Singleton EJB with a timer method ensures only one instance handles the scheduled task, preventing duplicate execution
C) @Stateless — stateless session beans are the recommended container type for all periodic timer-based tasks
D) @Stateful — stateful beans preserve execution context between successive scheduled invocation calls by design
questions.explanation: Timer EJBs use @Singleton (one instance per app) combined with @Schedule to define scheduled tasks. This prevents multiple instances running the same cleanup job concurrently.

questions.question: SOAP's core purpose is to:
options: A) SOAP compresses XML payloads using GZIP encoding to speed up web service calls over bandwidth-limited networks
B) SOAP authenticates and authorises callers across distributed systems using the WS-Security header standards
C) Define a standardised XML envelope for application-to-application messages
D) SOAP replaces the HTTP protocol with a faster binary framing transport layer modelled on the BEEP protocol
questions.explanation: SOAP defines a standardised XML message format for communication between distributed systems, regardless of platform or language.

questions.question: WSDL is an XML language used to:
options: A) Configures load balancing rules and failover strategies across multiple service provider endpoint instances
B) Describe a SOAP service: operations, messages, protocol binding, and endpoint location
C) Defines JSF navigation rules and managed bean registration entries inside the web application descriptor
D) Stores runtime configuration properties and environment-specific deployment settings for a web service
questions.explanation: WSDL formally describes a SOAP web service: operations, message types, protocol bindings, and where to reach it.

questions.question: SOAP web services are preferred over REST when:
options: A) The client is a browser making lightweight Ajax calls that benefit from compact JSON over verbose SOAP envelopes
B) Building a fast, simple public API for a mobile application that has strict payload size and latency requirements
C) The team wants minimal tooling and documentation overhead, favouring agility over formal service contracts
D) Security, reliability, and formal WSDL contracts are critical (e.g., banking systems)
questions.explanation: SOAP is chosen for enterprise environments — financial, insurance — where WS-Security, reliable messaging, and formal WSDL contracts are required.

questions.question: The bottom-up SOAP development strategy starts with:
options: A) A WSDL contract defining all operations first — Java implementation code is then generated from the WSDL
B) An annotated Java class — the WSDL is generated from the code
C) An OpenAPI specification document — the Java service implementation is scaffolded from the OpenAPI YAML
D) A database schema — service operations and their data types are inferred directly from the table structure
questions.explanation: Bottom-up (code-first): start with annotated Java, generate WSDL automatically. Easiest approach when exposing an existing class as a web service.

questions.question: In a WSDL document, the 'portType' element defines:
options: A) The concrete service endpoint URL address where clients must send their SOAP HTTP request messages
B) The TCP port number on which the service listens for incoming SOAP-over-HTTP requests from remote clients
C) The abstract interface — the operations the service supports
D) The XML data format and schema types that define the structure of messages exchanged with the service
questions.explanation: portType is the abstract interface section of a WSDL, listing operations and their input/output/fault messages independent of protocol.

questions.question: JAX-WS uses which companion spec to map Java objects to and from XML in SOAP messages?
options: A) JPA — the Jakarta Persistence API used for object-relational mapping and entity lifecycle management
B) Jakarta XML Binding (JAXB)
C) JSON-B — the Jakarta JSON Binding API for bidirectional mapping between Java objects and JSON documents
D) JSON-P — the Jakarta JSON Processing API for event-driven parsing and building of JSON document structures
questions.explanation: JAX-WS works with JAXB to handle bidirectional mapping between Java objects and the XML payloads in SOAP messages.

options: A) @WebMethod generates an additional JAX-RS REST endpoint URL mapping for the 'add' method in parallel
B) @WebService exposes the class as a SOAP endpoint; @WebMethod marks 'add' as a web service operation — it will appear as an operation in the generated WSDL
C) @WebMethod restricts the 'add' method to private visibility so external SOAP callers cannot invoke it
D) Without @WebMethod all public methods are auto-exposed; adding @WebMethod actually excludes 'add' from the WSDL portType
questions.explanation: @WebService exposes the class as a SOAP web service endpoint. @WebMethod explicitly marks the 'add' method as a web service operation, which will appear in the generated WSDL's portType as an operation.

questions.question: Jakarta Bean Validation lets you express constraints using:
options: A) JavaScript validation rules executed only in the browser, with no enforcement performed on the server side
B) SQL CHECK constraints defined in the database schema, enforcing data rules only at the persistence layer
C) JPA column definition attributes like nullable=false that apply only during DDL schema generation at deploy time
D) Annotations or XML on JavaBean fields, methods, and constructors
questions.explanation: Bean Validation uses metadata — annotations or XML — applied to JavaBeans, their attributes, constructors, and method parameters/return values.

questions.question: Which predefined constraint ensures a field is never null?
options: A) @NotEmpty
B) @NotNull
C) @NotBlank
D) @Required
questions.explanation: @NotNull from jakarta.validation.constraints ensures the annotated element is not null.

questions.question: In a JAX-RS resource method, adding @Valid to a parameter causes the runtime to:
options: A) Validates only the JSON structure and syntax of the incoming payload, ignoring individual field constraint values
B) Run Bean Validation and return HTTP 400 if a constraint fails
C) Logs the incoming parameter value to the server's audit trail without performing any validation checks at all
D) Bypass validation entirely and trust the client-provided data
questions.explanation: @Valid triggers Bean Validation. If a constraint is violated, the JAX-RS runtime returns HTTP 400 Bad Request automatically.

questions.question: What does Validator.validate() return when ALL constraints pass?
options: A) An empty Set
B) A ConstraintViolation with status OK
C) true — a primitive boolean value indicating all validations passed without any constraint violations found
D) null — a null reference returned to indicate that validation was skipped or the parameter was not checked
questions.explanation: validate() returns a Set<ConstraintViolation<T>>. An empty set means all constraints passed; non-empty means violations were found.

questions.question: Bean Validation constraints CANNOT be placed on which of the following?
options: A) Package declarations
B) Constructor parameters
C) Class field declarations annotated with standard field-level constraint annotations like @NotNull
D) Method return values
questions.explanation: Constraints apply to beans, fields, constructors, method parameters, and return values — but not package declarations.

questions.question: Which predefined constraint limits the number of characters in a String or elements in a collection?
options: A) @Length
B) @Size
C) @MaxSize — not a valid Jakarta Bean Validation constraint annotation in the specification
D) @Limit
questions.explanation: @Size(min, max) constrains the size of a String (character count), Collection, Map, or array.

options: A) new Book() with title=null, price=5.0f, description="A great book"
B) new Book() with title="Clean Code", price=29.99f, description=null
C) new Book() with title="Refactoring", price=15.0f, description="" — an empty string satisfies @Size(max=2000)
D) new Book() with title="DDD", price=3.0f, description=null — a null description is not constrained by @Size
questions.explanation: title=null violates @NotNull on the title field. price=5.0f passes @Min(2). A null description is allowed (only @Size constrains length, not nullability). Option B fails because title is null.

questions.question: Jakarta Faces (JSF) is built on which design pattern?
options: A) Observer design pattern — where components react to state changes in other objects they subscribe to
B) Model-View-Controller (MVC)
C) Factory design pattern — providing a single interface for creating families of related UI component objects
D) Singleton design pattern — ensuring that only one shared instance of the view state object ever exists
questions.explanation: JSF follows MVC: FacesServlet is the controller, JSF pages are the view, and managed beans form the model.

questions.question: In the JSF MVC model, FacesServlet acts as the:
options: A) DAO — persisting form data submitted to the database via the JPA EntityManager persistence context API
B) Controller — processing requests and managing the page lifecycle
C) Model — holding the business data that is bound and rendered by EL expressions inside Facelets templates
D) View — rendering the final HTML markup output to the browser by walking the JSF component tree directly
questions.explanation: FacesServlet is the front controller in JSF. It intercepts all Faces requests, runs the six-phase lifecycle, and delegates to the appropriate view and backing beans.

questions.question: Why is Facelets preferred over JSP in JSF applications?
options: A) Facelets compiles XHTML templates to optimised binary bytecode representation at build time for performance
B) Facelets uses a JSON-based template format instead of XML for declaring and nesting UI component hierarchies
C) Facelets eliminates the need for managed beans by allowing all business logic to be inlined in the template
D) Facelets uses XHTML, offering better templating, error reporting, and composition than JSP
questions.explanation: Facelets uses XHTML to describe JSF component trees, offering better error reporting, templating, and component composition than the older JSP approach.

questions.question: Expression Language (EL) expressions like #{bean.property} in a Facelets page:
options: A) Generates a parameterised SQL query dynamically at render time, treating the expression as a named query key
B) Executes a client-side JavaScript function in the browser DOM when the page finishes loading completely
C) Bind component values and actions to managed bean properties and methods
D) Applies an inline CSS style rule directly to the specific JSF component element during the render response phase
questions.explanation: EL expressions two-way-bind JSF components to managed bean properties (reads/writes) and wire action components to backing bean methods.

questions.question: The JSF page lifecycle has exactly how many phases?
options: A) Eight
B) Six
C) Four
D) Three
questions.explanation: JSF has six lifecycle phases: Restore View, Apply Request Values, Process Validations, Update Model Values, Invoke Application, and Render Response.

questions.question: Global navigation in JSF is configured via:
options: A) URL path mappings configured inside the persistence.xml file alongside named persistence unit definitions
B) Navigation rules declared inside web.xml using <navigation-case> XML elements mapped to servlet URL patterns
C) @NavigationRule annotations on managed beans
D) Navigation rules in faces-config.xml
questions.explanation: Global navigation uses <navigation-rule> elements in faces-config.xml to map outcome strings from action methods to destination pages.

questions.question: In the JSF MVC pattern, managed beans form the:
options: A) Controller — FacesServlet delegates HTTP request processing directly to managed bean action methods
B) Persistence layer — managed beans are responsible for storing and retrieving entity data from the database
C) Model — holding business data and logic that views bind to
D) View — managed beans generate the final rendered HTML markup by producing JSF component output directly
questions.explanation: Managed beans are the Model in JSF MVC. They hold data displayed by views and expose action methods invoked by user interactions.

questions.question:
In this JSF Facelets snippet, what does #{credentials.username} do?

<h:inputText id="userName"
  value="#{credentials.username}" />
options: A) It outputs the literal text string 'credentials.username' unchanged as static content in the rendered HTML page
B) It invokes a method named exactly 'username()' as an action listener callback on the credentials bean instance
C) It directs CDI to instantiate a new managed bean named 'credentials' with a field declaration called 'username'
D) It binds the input field's value to the 'username' property of the 'credentials' managed bean using EL
questions.explanation: #{credentials.username} is an EL expression that two-way-binds the input field: JSF reads the bean property to pre-fill the field and writes the submitted value back to the bean property.

options: A) return new FacesResponse("dashboard"); — wrapping the outcome string inside a FacesResponse navigation wrapper
B) return "redirect:/dashboard"; — using the Spring MVC redirect prefix convention for JSF navigation outcomes
C) return "dashboard"; — JSF uses the returned String as the navigation outcome to find or navigate to dashboard.xhtml
D) return null; — null is always interpreted by JSF as a forward to the next page in the configured sequence
questions.explanation: In JSF, an action method returns a String outcome. Returning 'dashboard' tells the JSF navigation system to navigate to 'dashboard.xhtml' (explicit navigation). Returning null re-renders the current page.

questions.question: JPA solves the 'object-relational impedance mismatch' by:
options: A) Auto-generating REST endpoints directly from entity class definitions
B) Replacing the JDBC driver entirely with a NoSQL-compatible data access layer that wraps existing JPA APIs
C) Automatically migrating relational SQL databases to document-oriented NoSQL storage at application startup
D) Annotating plain Java classes to map them to relational tables (ORM)
questions.explanation: JPA provides annotations like @Entity, @Id, @Column to map POJOs to relational tables, bridging the gap between object-oriented Java and relational databases.

questions.question: Which annotation turns a plain Java class into a JPA entity?
options: A) @DataObject
B) @Table — only specifies the table name mapping hint, but does not make the class a JPA-managed entity
C) @Entity
D) @Persistent
questions.explanation: @Entity marks a POJO as a persistent entity that JPA maps to a database table.

questions.question: The EntityManager is central to JPA because it is:
options: A) The web-layer session manager that tracks authenticated users and stores their HTTP session state objects
B) The CDI injection handler responsible for locating and providing managed bean instances to application code
C) The main API for persisting, finding, updating, removing, and querying entities
D) The EJB lifecycle controller managing session bean creation, pooling, passivation, activation, and destruction
questions.explanation: EntityManager provides persist(), find(), merge(), remove() and query methods — it is the developer's primary interface to the persistence layer.

questions.question: Which annotation injects a container-managed EntityManager?
options: A) @PersistenceContext
B) @Inject — intended for general CDI beans, not the standard way to inject a container-managed EntityManager
C) @Autowired — a Spring Framework annotation with no equivalent meaning in the Jakarta EE CDI specification
D) @Resource — used for JNDI resource injection but not the standard annotation for EntityManager injection
questions.explanation: @PersistenceContext injects a container-managed EntityManager tied to the named persistence unit in persistence.xml.

questions.question: How would you annotate the 'customer' side of a one-customer-to-many-orders relationship in JPA?
options: A) @ManyToOne
B) @OneToMany
C) @OneToOne — maps a relationship where one entity instance relates to exactly one other entity instance
D) @ManyToMany
questions.explanation: @OneToMany on the Customer entity maps the relationship where one Customer can be associated with many Order entities.

questions.question: The JPA Persistence Context is:
options: A) A set of managed entity instances tracked by the EntityManager
B) A compiled second-level SQL query cache used by the JPA provider to avoid repeated query compilation overhead
C) The persistence.xml configuration file holding the datasource JNDI name, provider class, and DDL properties
D) The underlying JDBC connection pool configured in the application server and referenced by the persistence unit
questions.explanation: The Persistence Context is an in-memory set of managed entity instances. The EntityManager tracks changes and synchronises with the database at transaction commit.

questions.question: JPQL differs from SQL in that it:
options: A) It supports only SELECT read queries — bulk INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements are completely unsupported
B) It compiles queries directly into database stored procedures, eliminating any JDBC round-trip overhead entirely
C) It is always faster than native SQL because it avoids any additional network round trips to the database server
D) It queries entity objects and fields, not database tables or column names
questions.explanation: JPQL (Java Persistence Query Language) works against the entity model — you write queries against class names and field names, not table/column names.

questions.question: A common JPA pitfall with lazy loading is:
options: A) Unexpected SQL queries or exceptions when lazy associations are accessed outside a transaction
B) A compile-time error is raised whenever a lazy-loaded association field is accessed outside the entity class
C) The primary key value is silently regenerated every time the entity is read back from the persistence context
D) The JPA provider automatically and transparently upgrades the association from LAZY to EAGER fetch type
questions.explanation: Lazy-loaded associations are fetched on access. Outside an active persistence context this causes LazyInitializationException or N+1 query problems.

questions.question: @GeneratedValue on a JPA @Id field instructs the provider to:
options: A) Creates the primary key database column definition only during DDL schema generation, not on individual persists
B) Produces a new UUID for the @Id field on every single read operation performed against the persistence context
C) Automatically generate the primary key value when a new entity is persisted
D) Validates that the @Id field contains a non-null value before the INSERT SQL statement is sent to the database
questions.explanation: @GeneratedValue specifies the PK generation strategy (IDENTITY, SEQUENCE, TABLE, or AUTO) so developers don't need to set IDs manually.

questions.question: @Column(length = 200) in JPA primarily affects:
options: A) The maximum character length enforced during JSON-B serialization — values exceeding 200 are silently truncated
B) The maximum number of characters the EntityManager reads back from the database column into the Java field
C) The generated database column definition — sets VARCHAR length to 200
D) A runtime Bean Validation constraint — values exceeding 200 characters throw a ConstraintViolationException
questions.explanation: @Column(length=200) is a DDL hint — it sets the column's VARCHAR(200) definition in the schema generated by JPA.

questions.question: Which component in the JPA architecture creates EntityManager instances?
options: A) Persistence (bootstrap class)
B) EntityManagerFactory
C) PersistenceUnit — the named configuration unit, not the factory responsible for creating EntityManagers
D) persistence.xml — the configuration descriptor file, not a runtime component that instantiates EntityManagers
questions.explanation: EntityManagerFactory creates EntityManager instances. It is configured by a PersistenceUnit defined in persistence.xml.

options: A) A JPA entity mapped to a BOOK table with columns: ID (bigint, primary key, auto-generated), TITLE (varchar 200), and PRICE (double)
B) A JPA entity mapped to a BOOK table where all three columns are declared NOT NULL by default at schema generation time
C) A JPA entity where 'id' is stored as a String UUID primary key and 'price' is persisted as a text VARCHAR column
D) A non-persistent plain Java class named Book with no database table mapping because it lacks a required @Table annotation
questions.explanation: @Entity maps the class to a BOOK table. @Id @GeneratedValue makes ID the auto-generated primary key. @Column(length=200) limits TITLE to varchar(200). PRICE maps to a double/float column. Columns are nullable by default.

options: A) It reads an existing Book entity from the database by its primary key value using EntityManager.find(Book.class, id)
B) It merges and updates an existing detached Book record in the database using the EntityManager.merge(book) operation
C) It injects an EntityManager and uses it to insert (persist) a new Book entity into the database within the current transaction
D) It removes the Book entity from both the persistence context and the database using EntityManager.remove(book)
questions.explanation: @PersistenceContext injects an EntityManager tied to the 'simplePU' persistence unit. em.persist(book) makes the Book entity managed and schedules it for INSERT into the database when the transaction commits.

options: A) @PersistenceContext injection is invalid in a plain Java class — only EJBs and CDI beans support container injection, so Persistence.createEntityManagerFactory() must be used manually
B) Nothing is wrong with this code whatsoever — it is perfectly valid JPA, and both the repository class and the Book class are correctly structured for any compliant JPA provider
C) The saveBook() method must be annotated with @Transactional for any JPA write operation to succeed, since JPA always requires an active transaction to call em.persist() without exception
D) The Book class is missing the @Entity annotation — without it, JPA does not treat Book as a persistent entity and em.persist() will throw an exception
questions.explanation: Without @Entity on the Book class, JPA does not recognize it as a mapped entity. em.persist() requires the argument to be an @Entity-annotated class. The fix is to add @Entity (and @Id on the id field) to Book.

questions.question: REST stands for:
options: A) Representational State Transfer
B) Reliable Enterprise Service Transport
C) Resource Endpoint Service Technology
D) Remote Execution and State Transfer
questions.explanation: REST (Representational State Transfer) is a stateless, HTTP-based architectural style for building web services.

questions.question: Which JAX-RS annotation maps a class or method to a URI path?
options: A) @Route
B) @Endpoint
C) @Path
D) @URL — a non-existent JAX-RS annotation; no such annotation exists in the Jakarta RESTful specification
questions.explanation: @Path defines the URI segment for a resource class or method, e.g., @Path('users') maps to /api/users.

questions.question: Which HTTP method should be used to create a new resource?
options: A) POST
B) DELETE
C) GET — used to read and retrieve an existing resource without making any modifications to server state
D) PUT — used to replace or fully update an existing resource at a known URI with new content entirely
questions.explanation: POST maps to Create in CRUD conventions. For example, POST /users creates a new user.

questions.question: Which HTTP method should be used to retrieve an existing resource without modifying it?
options: A) PUT
B) GET
C) PATCH
D) POST
questions.explanation: GET maps to Read in CRUD. It is idempotent and must not change server state.

questions.question: @PathParam in JAX-RS extracts:
options: A) A named value extracted from an HTTP request header field, such as Authorization or Accept-Language
B) A value parsed from the URL query string (e.g., the '5' in the query parameter ?id=5 after the '?')
C) A value decoded from the HTTP request body, typically a form field in an application/x-www-form-urlencoded payload
D) A segment variable from the URI path template (e.g., /users/{userId})
questions.explanation: @PathParam maps a URI path template variable directly to a Java method parameter.

questions.question: Response.created(uri) in JAX-RS sets the HTTP status code to:
options: A) 204 No Content
B) 200 OK — the standard success response code indicating the request was processed without any issues
C) 201 Created
D) 302 Found — a redirect response code sending the client to a different location URL for the resource
questions.explanation: Response.created(uri) returns HTTP 201 Created and sets the Location header to the URI of the newly created resource.

questions.question: A JAX-RS ExceptionMapper lets you:
options: A) Log the caught exception details to a rotating log file appender configured in the server logging setup
B) Automatically retry the failed HTTP request after a configurable back-off delay before returning an error
C) Transparently redirect the browser client to a dedicated HTML error page via a 302 redirect response
D) Map a thrown exception to a specific HTTP response
questions.explanation: ExceptionMapper<T> catches a specific exception thrown during processing and converts it into a tailored HTTP Response (status code + body).

questions.question: Server-Sent Events (SSE) in JAX-RS provides:
options: A) Unidirectional server-to-client real-time push over a single persistent HTTP connection
B) Provides a fully bidirectional push channel where both client and server can send data frames freely at will
C) Implements a polling mechanism where the client automatically checks for new data on a short fixed interval
D) Enables client-to-server unidirectional streaming over a WebSocket-like upgrade of the HTTP connection
questions.explanation: SSE enables servers to push events to clients over a single long-lived HTTP connection in one direction only. JAX-RS supports it via SseEventSink and Sse.

questions.question: @BeanParam in JAX-RS is used to:
options: A) Injects the full HttpServletRequest object as a CDI-managed bean directly into the resource method parameter
B) Automatically deserialises the incoming JSON request body into a Java object using JSON-B conversion rules
C) Group multiple parameter annotations into a single request object
D) Triggers Jakarta Bean Validation constraints on the request body by running all annotated field-level checks
questions.explanation: @BeanParam aggregates @QueryParam, @PathParam, @HeaderParam etc. into a single encapsulating class, keeping method signatures clean.

questions.question: The JAX-RS client API centres around which interface?
options: A) ClientRequest
B) HttpClient
C) WebTarget
D) RestClient
questions.explanation: The JAX-RS client API is built around jakarta.ws.rs.client.WebTarget. You chain paths and parameters on a WebTarget then invoke HTTP methods.

options: A) GET to /users — retrieves and returns a paginated JSON array listing every user registered in the system
B) GET to /users/{userId} (e.g., /users/42) — retrieves the user with that ID and returns it serialized as JSON
C) POST to /users — accepts a JSON request body to create and persist a new user, then returns the created record
D) DELETE to /users/{userId} — permanently removes the identified user from the data store with no response body
questions.explanation: @GET means it handles HTTP GET. @Path("{userId}") appends a variable segment to the class-level path. @PathParam injects the ID. The UserResponse return type is automatically serialized to JSON (as defined by @Produces on the class).

options: A) It configures all JAX-RS resources to only accept requests whose Content-Type header is application/json
B) It overrides the entire web application context root to 'api', changing the deployment path of all servlets
C) It defines the base URI path '/api' for all JAX-RS resources — e.g., a resource at @Path('users') will be at /api/users
D) It maps the whole application to a fixed port number (8080), overriding the default container port binding
questions.explanation: @ApplicationPath defines the base path for all JAX-RS resources in the application. With @ApplicationPath("api"), a resource class annotated @Path("users") will be reachable at /api/users.

options: A) 302 Found; the Location header redirects the calling client to the paginated user list endpoint for browsing
B) 201 Created; Location header contains the full URI of the newly created user (e.g., http://host/api/users/42)
C) 204 No Content; the Location header echoes back the original request URI but the response body is empty
D) 200 OK; no Location header is included because standard success responses do not carry resource location URIs
questions.explanation: Response.created(uri) sets HTTP 201 Created and automatically sets the Location header to the provided URI — in this case the absolute path of the new user resource. The entity (user object) is also returned in the response body.

questions.question: The two JSON-P approaches for working with JSON data are:
options: A) Object Model API (in-memory tree) and Streaming API (event-based)
B) A Serialization API for producing JSON output and a separate Deserialization API for consuming JSON input
C) A DOM API that builds an in-memory XML-compatible node tree and an XPath API for navigating JSON paths
D) A Push API that emits parse events and a Pull API where the application explicitly requests the next token
questions.explanation: JSON-P offers the Object Model API (full in-memory tree) and the Streaming API (token-by-token, lower memory).

questions.question: The JSON-P Streaming API is preferred when:
options: A) The JSON document is very small (under 1 KB) and can trivially fit in memory without any performance concern
B) The JSON document is very large and full in-memory loading is impractical
C) You need to randomly navigate and read many scattered parts of a small fully in-memory JSON document tree
D) You require JSON Pointer addressing or JSON Patch operations that depend on a fully addressable document tree
questions.explanation: The Streaming API processes JSON sequentially without a full in-memory tree — ideal for large documents where memory footprint matters.

questions.question: Jakarta JSON Binding (JSON-B) automatically:
options: A) Validates a JSON input document against a JSON Schema specification file, reporting any structural violations
B) Streams very large JSON files from disk using a buffered token reader to avoid loading them entirely in memory
C) Transforms a JSON document into an equivalent XML representation suitable for XSLT processing pipeline use
D) Serializes Java objects to JSON and deserializes JSON back to Java objects
questions.explanation: JSON-B provides toJson() and fromJson() on the Jsonb interface for automatic bidirectional mapping between Java objects and JSON.

questions.question: What does @JsonbProperty('person-name') achieve on a Java field named 'name'?
options: A) It marks the JSON property as required — JSON-B throws a deserialization exception if the key is absent
B) It renames the Java field descriptor permanently in the compiled bytecode, changing the internal field name
C) It maps the Java field 'name' to the JSON key 'person-name'
D) It excludes the annotated field entirely from both JSON serialization output and deserialization input parsing
questions.explanation: @JsonbProperty customises the JSON key name. With @JsonbProperty('person-name'), the 'name' field serialises to 'person-name' in JSON.

questions.question: Which JSON-B annotation excludes a field from both serialization and deserialization?
options: A) @JsonbExclude — this annotation name does not exist anywhere in the Jakarta JSON-B specification API
B) @JsonbTransient
C) @JsonbSkip — this annotation name is not defined or valid in any version of the JSON-B specification
D) @JsonbIgnore — this annotation name does not exist in the Jakarta JSON-B specification as a valid API type
questions.explanation: @JsonbTransient marks a field to be excluded entirely from JSON-B processing.

questions.question: To create a Jsonb instance in JSON-B you use:
options: A) JsonbBuilder.create() (optionally with a JsonbConfig)
B) Jsonb.getInstance() — a fictional static factory method that does not exist on the Jsonb interface at all
C) JsonbFactory.newInstance() — a fictional dedicated factory class patterned after the JAXB JAXBContext API
D) new Jsonb() — direct instantiation via a public constructor, which is not possible as Jsonb is an interface
questions.explanation: JsonbBuilder.create() returns a Jsonb instance. Pass a JsonbConfig for custom configuration (ordering, adapters, etc.).

questions.question: JSON Patch (JsonPatch) in JSON-P allows you to:
options: A) Compresses a JSON document by stripping all whitespace characters and then applying GZIP byte encoding
B) Converts an in-memory JSON document into an equivalent XML document representation for further processing
C) Applies a runtime security patch to the JSON-P library implementation to fix known parser vulnerabilities
D) Perform targeted operations (add, remove, replace, move, copy) on a JSON document
questions.explanation: JsonPatch applies a sequence of operations to a JSON document — you can add, remove, replace, move, or copy values without replacing the entire document.

options: A) name=Falco&age=4&bitable=false — URL-encoded form parameter format rather than a JSON object representation
B) {"Dog":{"name":"Falco","age":4,"bitable":false}}
C) {"name":"Falco","age":4,"bitable":false}
D) ["Falco", 4, false] — a JSON array of positional values with no property name keys or object wrapper
questions.explanation: JSON-B's default mapping serializes the public fields directly to a flat JSON object using field names as keys. The class name is not included as a wrapping key.

options: A) {"id":1,"person-name":"Jhon","registeredDate":"07-09-2019"}
B) {"Person":{"id":1,"name":"Jhon","registeredDate":"07-09-2019"}}
C) {"id":1,"name":"Jhon","age":20,"registeredDate":"2019-09-07"}
D) {"id":1,"person-name":"Jhon","age":20,"registeredDate":"07-09-2019"}
questions.explanation: @JsonbProperty renames 'name' to 'person-name'. @JsonbTransient excludes 'age' entirely. @JsonbDateFormat('dd-MM-yyyy') formats the date as '07-09-2019'. The result has no 'age' field and uses the custom name and date format.

questions.question: Jakarta Messaging (JMS) enables applications to communicate:
options: A) Via shared database tables that both the producing and consuming side poll continuously for pending work
B) Through direct RMI (Remote Method Invocation) calls that tightly couple the two communicating JVM processes
C) Synchronously via HTTP REST calls, where the message consumer blocks the producer thread until completion
D) Asynchronously via a messaging provider, decoupling producers from consumers
questions.explanation: JMS provides asynchronous, loosely coupled message exchange. Producers send to destinations; consumers receive without needing to be online simultaneously.

questions.question: In the Point-to-Point model, each queue message is consumed by:
options: A) All consumers registered within the same JVM share delivery in a local round-robin loop across threads
B) All registered subscribers on the queue receive a full copy of every published message simultaneously
C) The first available consumer — exactly once
D) The JMS provider itself consumes the message for internal dead-letter queue routing and redelivery purposes
questions.explanation: P2P uses a queue. Each message is delivered to exactly one consumer — even if multiple consumers listen on the same queue.

questions.question: In the Publish/Subscribe model, a message published to a topic is delivered to:
options: A) Only subscribers running inside the same application on the same application server node will receive it
B) All currently registered subscribers on that topic
C) Only the very first subscriber that polls the topic after the message is published will receive that copy
D) Exactly one consumer is chosen by the JMS provider in a round-robin delivery rotation for each sent message
questions.explanation: In Pub/Sub, a topic is a broadcast destination — every active subscriber receives a copy of each published message.

questions.question: Which EJB type is specifically designed to consume JMS messages asynchronously?
options: A) Stateful Session Bean — maintains conversational state per specific client across multiple method calls
B) Singleton EJB — exactly one shared instance per application, not triggered by JMS message arrival
C) Stateless Session Bean — pooled with no per-client state, not designed for JMS message consumption
D) Message-Driven Bean (MDB)
questions.explanation: MDBs implement MessageListener and are activated by the container when a JMS message arrives, making them the standard async message consumers in Jakarta EE.

questions.question: An MDB class must implement which interface?
options: A) jakarta.messaging.Consumer — a fictional higher-level interface not defined in the JMS specification API
B) jakarta.jms.MessageListener
C) jakarta.ejb.MessageReceiver
D) jakarta.jms.MessageHandler — a fictional functional variant interface not present in the JMS specification
questions.explanation: jakarta.jms.MessageListener defines the single onMessage(Message) method that the container calls when a message is available.

questions.question: @JMSDestinationDefinition on an MDB class tells the server to:
options: A) Sends an automatic test message to the configured queue at server startup to verify the destination is reachable
B) Instructs the container to automatically inject a JMSContext into any bean field annotated with @Inject
C) Limits the maximum number of concurrent message consumer threads that the container allocates to this MDB
D) Declare the JMS destination as a resource, creating it if it does not exist
questions.explanation: @JMSDestinationDefinition declares the queue or topic as a named resource. If the destination doesn't exist, the app server creates it at deployment.

questions.question: In the JMS exercise, what component actually sends messages to the queue?
options: A) TestServlet via a raw JMS API call using directly instantiated ConnectionFactory and Session object instances
B) The Payara admin console's built-in 'Send Test Message' function available on the queue management screen
C) The @Stateless MessageProducer EJB, injected into TestServlet via @EJB
D) The MDB's onMessage() callback, which sends a reply back to the originating source destination on completion
questions.explanation: MessageProducer is a @Stateless EJB injected into TestServlet. Its sendMessage() uses the injected JMSContext and Queue to dispatch the message.

questions.question: JMS Message Selectors let consumers:
options: A) Choose which JMS provider or message broker to connect to based on the configured JNDI connection factory name
B) Throttle the message delivery rate from the broker to prevent a slow consumer from being overwhelmed
C) Select the serialisation format (text, bytes, or object) applied to the content body of incoming messages
D) Filter which messages they receive, based on message header or property criteria
questions.explanation: Message selectors work like SQL WHERE clauses on message properties — only matching messages are delivered to that consumer.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a valid JMS message type?
options: A) BinaryMessage
B) ObjectMessage
C) BytesMessage — a valid JMS type for raw binary content
D) TextMessage — a valid JMS type for String content
questions.explanation: JMS defines TextMessage, ObjectMessage, BytesMessage, MapMessage, and StreamMessage. 'BinaryMessage' does not exist.

questions.question: A JMS Connection Factory is responsible for:
options: A) Stores all undelivered messages persistently in a durable store until a matching consumer connects and acknowledges
B) Creating JMS Connections between a client and the messaging provider
C) Routes messages between different queues and topics using content-based routing rules configured at the broker
D) Transforms outbound JMS messages into equivalent REST HTTP calls for integration with non-JMS external systems
questions.explanation: A Connection Factory is an administered object that creates JMS Connection instances — the communication channels between a client and the broker.

options: A) It listens on the simpleQ queue and, whenever a message arrives, logs its text content via onMessage()
