B) It sends a brand new text message to the simpleQ queue each time the onMessage() method body is triggered
C) It programmatically creates the simpleQ queue destination on the broker if it does not already exist there
D) It polls the simpleQ queue on a configurable schedule interval (default every 5 seconds) for pending messages
questions.explanation: @MessageDriven with activationConfig binds this MDB to the simpleQ queue. The container calls onMessage() automatically whenever a message is available. The body is read as a String and logged.

options: A) The servlet ignores the 'message' URL query parameter entirely and always dispatches the 'Hello JMS!' default
B) The servlet reads 'Hi!' from the URL, calls producer.sendMessage('Hi!') to put it in the JMS queue, then responds with 'Message sent!' and the current date
C) The servlet writes 'Hi!' directly into the HTTP response body, completely bypassing the JMS message queue
D) The servlet throws an IllegalArgumentException because 'message' is not a recognised standard HTTP header name
questions.explanation: ofNullable(...).orElse('Hello JMS!') reads the 'message' query param (here 'Hi!') or defaults to 'Hello JMS!'. producer.sendMessage() dispatches it to the JMS queue via the injected EJB. The response confirms the send.

options: A) JMSContext is a CDI conversation-scoped proxy that batches all pending messages until the scope boundary closes
B) context.createProducer() creates a JMS MessageProducer; .send(queue, msg) sends the String as a TextMessage to the simpleQ queue via the injected JMSContext
C) @Inject JMSContext opens a raw TCP socket connection directly to the broker; send() serialises bytes over it
D) @Resource injects the raw message text payload as a String field; send() merely logs it without broker delivery
questions.explanation: JMSContext is a simplified API (JMS 2.0+) that combines Connection and Session. createProducer() builds a fluent producer and .send(queue, msg) dispatches the String as a JMS TextMessage to the specified queue destination.

questions.question: Jakarta Batch is designed for:
options: A) Bidirectional messaging between microservices using synchronous request-reply patterns over shared queues
B) Managing WebSocket connection pool lifecycles and broadcasting real-time events to all connected browsers
C) Bulk offline data processing — running jobs on large datasets
D) Handling real-time interactive web requests with low-latency response times for end-user-facing endpoints
questions.explanation: Jakarta Batch is a standard for batch processing: developing and running jobs that process large volumes of data, typically offline or scheduled.

questions.question: Jakarta Batch uses which language to define the structure and flow of a batch job?
options: A) Java annotations applied directly on step implementation classes to define job flow and step execution order
B) JSON — a structured data format used to declare batch job configuration in a portable machine-readable form
C) YAML — a human-readable hierarchical configuration format used to define the batch job execution structure
D) JSL — an XML-based Job Specification Language
questions.explanation: Jakarta Batch uses JSL (Job Specification Language), an XML dialect, to declare steps, flows, splits, and decision logic.

questions.question: A Chunk step in Jakarta Batch repeatedly:
options: A) Runs its body exactly once as an atomic task and immediately returns an exit status string to the runtime
B) Executes multiple independent processing steps in parallel by spawning a dedicated thread per step at runtime
C) Evaluates a conditional exit status and routes execution to the appropriate configured next step in the flow
D) Reads items from a source, processes each, and writes them to a destination
questions.explanation: A Chunk step loops: read (ItemReader) → process (ItemProcessor) → write (ItemWriter), handling items in configurable chunk sizes.

questions.question: A Batchlet step differs from a Chunk step in that it:
options: A) Can only source its input data from a relational database through a JDBC DataSource as the mandatory reader
B) Automatically runs in parallel with all other configured steps whenever the JSL declares a concurrent split
C) Is invoked once, runs to completion, and returns an exit status
D) Processes its input data in configurable chunk sizes using the iterative read-process-write loop of Chunk steps
questions.explanation: A Batchlet is a single atomic task — invoked once, runs to completion, and returns an exit status string via the Batchlet interface.

questions.question: Batch checkpoints exist to:
options: A) Pauses job execution entirely and suspends it in a waiting state pending manual human operator review
B) Validates each individual data item at intermediate processing points, collecting all violations before writing
C) Let the runtime save execution state, enabling restarts from the last checkpoint after failure
D) Marks explicit JTA transaction boundaries inside a step, triggering an independent database commit per interval
questions.explanation: Checkpoints allow the batch runtime to periodically save progress. On failure, the job restarts from the last checkpoint rather than the beginning.

questions.question: To start a batch job programmatically, a developer first:
options: A) Calls a static Job.start() factory method directly from any CDI bean, passing the job XML name as argument
B) Places a @BatchStart annotation on a CDI bean method that the container then automatically invokes at startup
C) Packages the job definitions in a separate .batch archive file and deploys it to a dedicated batch container
D) Gets a JobOperator from BatchRuntime, then calls operator.start()
questions.explanation: The API entry point is BatchRuntime.getJobOperator(). Its start(jobXmlName, properties) method kicks off job execution.

questions.question: The JSL 'split' element in Jakarta Batch allows:
options: A) Executing multiple flows in parallel
B) Distributing the job across multiple application server nodes
C) Splitting a large input file into smaller chunks
D) Dividing a step into parallel threads automatically
questions.explanation: The 'split' element groups multiple 'flow' elements that execute concurrently, enabling parallel processing of independent work streams.

questions.question: The JobRepository in Jakarta Batch stores:
options: A) Job execution state, history, and completion results
B) JSL XML job definition files for all deployed batch jobs, stored under the META-INF/batch-jobs directory
C) The current chunk step's in-flight buffered data items held in memory before the ItemWriter flush is triggered
D) Batch runtime configuration properties such as managed thread pool size and default checkpoint interval values
questions.explanation: JobRepository persists job execution information — state, step results, completion outcomes — enabling monitoring and controlled restarts.

questions.question:
In a Jakarta Batch Chunk step, three CDI beans are referenced. What is the role of each?

ItemReader  → reads items from a source
ItemProcessor → transforms/filters each item
ItemWriter  → writes processed items to a destination

If you are importing a CSV file into a database, which mapping is correct?
options: A) All three CDI beans are interchangeable and perform the same combined read-process-write function equivalently
B) ItemReader retrieves existing rows from the database; ItemProcessor validates each row; ItemWriter exports the validated rows to a new CSV output file on disk
C) ItemReader reads lines from the CSV file; ItemProcessor transforms/validates each row; ItemWriter inserts processed records into the database
D) ItemReader fetches records from the database using JPQL queries; ItemWriter persists results back to the database; ItemProcessor handles all CSV parsing and column mapping
questions.explanation: In a CSV-to-database import: ItemReader reads CSV lines, ItemProcessor validates/transforms them into domain objects, and ItemWriter bulk-inserts them into the database — this matches the chunk step's read-process-write pattern.

options: A) getJobExecution() is only callable after the job has reached a terminal COMPLETED or FAILED batch status state
B) Nothing is wrong — this is the correct way to start and monitor a batch job
C) BatchRuntime.getJobOperator() is not a valid method — you must use @Inject JobOperator
D) operator.start() requires the full file path to the JSL XML, not just the job name
questions.explanation: This is correct Jakarta Batch usage: BatchRuntime.getJobOperator() retrieves the JobOperator, start('myJob', props) launches the job defined in META-INF/batch-jobs/myJob.xml and returns an execution ID, and getJobExecution(id) lets you poll the batch status.

questions.question: The fundamental difference between WebSocket and HTTP is that WebSocket provides:
options: A) Faster HTTP request-response cycles via HTTP/2 server push frames that eliminate redundant new connection setup
B) UDP-based datagram delivery for lower-latency transmission, trading reliability for real-time throughput gains
C) Automatic per-message deflate compression of all frame payloads, reducing bandwidth for repetitive data streams
D) Full-duplex bidirectional communication over a single persistent TCP connection
questions.explanation: WebSocket establishes a persistent full-duplex channel — both client and server can send messages at any time, unlike HTTP's request-response model.

questions.question: A Java class becomes a server-side WebSocket endpoint by annotating it with:
options: A) @WebSocketServer
B) @SocketHandler — a non-existent annotation not defined in the Jakarta WebSocket specification at all
C) @ServerEndpoint
D) @WebEndpoint — a non-existent annotation name not present in the Jakarta WebSocket specification API
questions.explanation: @ServerEndpoint marks the class as a WebSocket server endpoint and specifies the connection URI.

questions.question: The @OnMessage annotation marks a method called when:
options: A) Fires when a new WebSocket handshake completes and a connection is successfully established with the client
B) A message arrives on the WebSocket connection
C) Fires when a WebSocket protocol error occurs or when an application exception propagates from a handler method
D) Fires when the WebSocket connection is gracefully closed by either the client or the server endpoint side
questions.explanation: @OnMessage marks the handler invoked whenever a text, binary, or pong message is received from the connected client.

questions.question: Which annotation marks the method called when a new WebSocket connection is established?
options: A) @OnClose
B) @OnMessage
C) @OnOpen
D) @OnConnect
questions.explanation: @OnOpen marks the method invoked when a client successfully opens a WebSocket connection to the endpoint.

questions.question: WebSocket is well-suited for chat and live dashboards because:
options: A) WebSocket eliminates the TCP three-way handshake entirely by upgrading an existing HTTP/2 multiplexed stream
B) WebSocket transmits data via UDP datagrams under the hood, trading delivery guarantees for real-time speed
C) WebSocket automatically applies per-frame deflate compression to every outbound text and binary message frame
D) Both client and server can push data at any time over the same persistent connection without repeated HTTP overhead
questions.explanation: The persistent full-duplex channel means either side can push data immediately without opening new connections or re-sending HTTP headers.

options: A) onOpen and onClose handle standard HTTP request/response lifecycle events; onMessage and onError handle only WebSocket binary frames
B) onOpen fires when a client connects; onMessage fires when a message arrives; onClose fires when the connection closes; onError fires when an error occurs
C) Only @OnMessage is a valid standard Jakarta WebSocket annotation; the remaining three are user-defined custom annotations
D) All four annotated methods handle the same message-received event but differ only in the Java parameter type accepted
questions.explanation: These are the four lifecycle annotations in Jakarta WebSocket: @OnOpen (connection established), @OnMessage (message received), @OnClose (connection closed), @OnError (error occurred). The endpoint is mapped to the /chat URI.

questions.question: Jakarta Security standardises HTTP authentication in web apps via:
options: A) JCA (Java Connector Architecture) resource adapters that plug custom authentication modules into the EIS tier
B) The HttpAuthenticationMechanism interface
C) JAAS login modules only, configured through server-specific proprietary login configuration files on the classpath
D) Servlet filters manually declared in web.xml that intercept every request and perform credential validation checks
questions.explanation: Jakarta Security standardises web authentication via HttpAuthenticationMechanism, providing built-in BASIC, FORM, Custom FORM, and OpenID Connect implementations.

questions.question: Which option is NOT one of the four built-in HttpAuthenticationMechanism types?
options: A) OAuth 1.0
B) OpenID Connect
C) FORM — one of the four built-in HttpAuthenticationMechanism implementations in the Jakarta Security spec
D) BASIC — one of the four built-in HttpAuthenticationMechanism implementations in the Jakarta Security spec
questions.explanation: The four built-in mechanisms are BASIC, FORM, Custom FORM, and OpenID Connect. OAuth 1.0 is not a built-in type.

questions.question: An Identity Store in Jakarta Security is responsible for:
options: A) Storing and providing access to user credentials and roles for authentication
B) Caches authenticated session tokens in a distributed in-memory store to avoid repeated credential verification
C) Encrypts and securely stores private key material and certificates inside a hardware security module on the host
D) Logs all failed login attempts and authentication errors to a persistent audit trail for compliance and forensics
questions.explanation: Identity Stores implement IdentityStore and serve as the source of truth for user credentials and roles — backed by a database, LDAP, or custom store.

questions.question: SecurityContext in Jakarta Security lets application code:
options: A) Programmatically query the authenticated caller, check their roles, or check web resource access
B) Configures TLS certificate keystores and truststores programmatically at runtime during application startup
C) Allows new HttpAuthenticationMechanism implementations to be registered dynamically without redeployment
D) Encrypts sensitive database column values transparently using a key derived from the authenticated caller's identity
questions.explanation: SecurityContext is an injectable CDI bean providing: getCallerPrincipal(), isCallerInRole(), and hasAccessToWebResource().

questions.question: OpenID Connect is built on top of which protocol?
options: A) SAML 2.0 — an XML-based assertion protocol for federated identity rather than an OAuth extension layer
B) LDAP — a directory access protocol for querying user stores, unrelated to the OAuth token flow at all
C) Kerberos — a symmetric-key ticket-based network authentication protocol not related to OAuth token grants
D) OAuth 2.0
questions.explanation: OpenID Connect (OIDC) is an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. It adds a JWT ID Token carrying user identity claims.

questions.question: A JWT (JSON Web Token) in OpenID Connect is:
options: A) An encrypted password digest sent to the client browser and stored inside a secured HttpOnly session cookie
B) A cryptographically signed JSON string containing identity claims (subject, groups, issuer, expiry)
C) A server-side session cookie mapped to an in-memory principal object managed by the servlet session store
D) A Base64-encoded serialised database row returned by the IdentityStore after a successful credential lookup
questions.explanation: A JWT is a compact, signed token. The OIDC ID Token carries claims like subject, groups, issuer, and expiry.

questions.question: Keycloak, referenced in the course, is:
options: A) A Jakarta Security built-in annotation that applies OAuth scope-based access control to JAX-RS REST endpoints
B) A JPA persistence provider like Hibernate, offering ORM mapping, query translation, and caching services
C) An open-source Identity and Access Management tool and OpenID Connect provider
D) A full Jakarta EE application server like Payara, providing a complete certified runtime for EE deployments
questions.explanation: Keycloak is an open-source IAM tool that acts as an OpenID Connect and OAuth 2.0 authorization server, centralising authentication and token issuance.

questions.question: In the Keycloak integration diagram, the Gateway Service:
options: A) Stores user credential data and group role memberships in a managed LDAP directory that backs the IdentityStore
B) Exposes protected REST endpoints, obtains access tokens from Keycloak, and propagates them to backend services
C) Represents the Keycloak authorisation server itself — the identity provider that issues access and ID tokens
D) Acts as the browser client that initiates the OpenID Connect authorisation code redirect flow to Keycloak
questions.explanation: The Gateway Service uses Jakarta Security with OpenID Connect to authenticate requests, obtains an access token from Keycloak, and forwards it when calling the System Service.

questions.question:
In the Jakarta Security diagram, what is the flow when an HTTP request arrives at a protected resource?

1. HTTP request arrives
2. ??? is invoked
3. Credentials checked against ???
4. If authenticated, ??? fetches roles
5. SecurityContext provides access info to the app
options: A) 2: HttpAuthenticationMechanism; 3: Identity Store (credential validation); 4: Identity Store (fetches roles)
B) 2: FacesServlet intercepts and routes the request; 3: persistence.xml provides data source credential details; 4: the CDI container resolves all bean scope boundaries
C) 2: IdentityStore validates the caller credentials directly; 3: HttpAuthenticationMechanism issues the challenge; 4: SecurityContext then dispatches the resolved roles
D) 2: SecurityContext intercepts the arriving request; 3: the database is queried directly over a JDBC connection; 4: the EntityManager manages the resulting principal object
questions.explanation: The flow is: HttpAuthenticationMechanism is invoked → it validates credentials against the IdentityStore → if authenticated, it fetches roles from the IdentityStore → SecurityContext makes this information available programmatically to the application.

options: A) @RolesAllowed is a standard CDI qualifier annotation used to select the correct alternative bean implementation at injection points
B) JsonWebToken must always be parsed manually from the raw Authorization Bearer header — @Inject does not apply to JWT token objects
C) @PermitAll means /hello-everyone is accessible to anyone (no token needed); @RolesAllowed means /hello-jwt requires a valid JWT token where the caller belongs to 'admin' or 'user' group
D) Both endpoints actually require a valid signed JWT to be called; @PermitAll only suppresses role checks but still enforces token authentication
questions.explanation: @PermitAll opens the endpoint to all callers with no authentication required. @RolesAllowed({'admin','user'}) requires a valid, cryptographically signed JWT token whose groups claim contains 'admin' or 'user'. The injected JsonWebToken gives access to token claims like subject.

questions.question: Arquillian is described as 'container-agnostic' because it:
options: A) Does not support any application server container and runs all tests in a plain JVM with fully mocked services
B) Works with many container types (GlassFish, WildFly, Payara, Tomcat, embedded, remote…)
C) Only integrates with Docker-managed containers and requires a running Docker daemon on the build machine
D) Creates a completely isolated virtual container instance per individual test method for maximum test isolation
questions.explanation: Arquillian abstracts container differences — the same test code runs against different containers by swapping the container adapter dependency.

questions.question: ShrinkWrap in Arquillian is used to:
options: A) Compresses the final packaged .war or .ear deployment artifact using ZIP deflate to reduce the file size
B) Declaratively build a micro-archive (WAR/JAR/EAR) in Java for test deployment
C) Replaces Apache Maven entirely as the project's build tool and dependency resolution engine for tests
D) Auto-generates mock CDI bean implementations from annotated interfaces for use in isolated unit tests
questions.explanation: ShrinkWrap provides a fluent Java API for creating deployment archives programmatically inside @Deployment methods, without a separate build step.

questions.question: Payara Micro is used in the Arquillian examples because it is:
options: A) A lightweight Jakarta EE server suited to microservices and embedded test execution
B) A Maven Surefire or Failsafe plugin specifically designed for integration testing Jakarta EE applications
C) A JavaScript-based test runtime built on Node.js that runs browser-side unit tests against JSF page components
D) A monitoring and metrics dashboard embedded in the application server to display live test execution results
questions.explanation: Payara Micro is a stripped-down version of Payara Server — fast to start and ideal for Arquillian embedded integration tests.

questions.question: In Arquillian's 'embedded' execution mode, the container:
options: A) Runs inside the same JVM as the test, managed by Arquillian
B) Runs on an already-started remote server that must be independently managed before the test suite is launched
C) Runs in a separately forked JVM process that Arquillian manages and communicates with over a local socket
D) Runs inside a pre-configured Docker container on the CI server requiring a mounted Docker socket to operate
questions.explanation: Embedded mode runs the container in the same JVM as the test process, giving Arquillian full control over its lifecycle.

questions.question: Arquillian provides test launchers for which two frameworks?
options: A) Mocha and Jasmine
B) PyTest and unittest
C) JUnit and TestNG
D) Cucumber and Selenium
questions.explanation: Arquillian supplies test runners for JUnit (including JUnit 5 via ArquillianExtension) and TestNG.

questions.question: How does Arquillian enrich test classes beyond plain unit tests?
options: A) It auto-generates mock stubs for every Jakarta EE API dependency that appears at any annotated injection point
B) It applies Spring Framework's @Autowired annotation to resolve and inject dependencies into test class fields
C) It supports only constructor-level injection — field and setter injection points are not enriched inside tests
D) It supports @Inject, @Resource, @EJB, and @PersistenceContext directly in test classes — just like production code
questions.explanation: Arquillian enriches test classes with full Jakarta EE injection support: @Inject, @Resource, @EJB, and @PersistenceContext all work in test methods.

questions.question: The Arquillian extensions Drone and Graphene are used for:
options: A) Web browser automation via Selenium WebDriver
B) Mocking CDI bean implementations in pure unit tests, replacing real dependencies with programmatic stubs
C) Measuring application throughput and response-time percentiles under configurable simulated concurrent load
D) Testing end-to-end JMS message flows, asserting messages are correctly routed to and consumed by MDBs
questions.explanation: Drone and Graphene integrate with Selenium WebDriver, enabling automated browser-based end-to-end testing of web UIs.

options: A) @Deployment marks the entire test class as a deployable component; ShrinkWrap is a standalone build tool that compiles Java source files into a runnable archive independently
B) @Deployment signals Arquillian to skip all container lifecycle management entirely and execute tests directly in the local JVM without any deployment step
C) ShrinkWrap is a complete drop-in replacement for Apache Maven that resolves all transitive dependency artifacts at test runtime from the local Maven repository

D) @Deployment marks a static method that builds the test archive (containing MyService and a beans.xml); Arquillian deploys this archive to the container before running tests, and @Inject works inside the test class
questions.explanation: @ExtendWith(ArquillianExtension.class) hooks Arquillian into JUnit 5. @Deployment builds the micro-archive (WAR with MyService + beans.xml) that Arquillian deploys to the container. @Inject then works in the test class just as in production code.

questions.question:
Looking at the Arquillian test execution flow with Payara Micro, what happens for EACH test class?

Unit test class
  → for each unit test class:
    1. Start Payara Micro and deploy archive
    2. Run test methods
    3. Stop Payara Micro
options: A) For each test class, Payara Micro starts fresh (deploying the archive), all test methods in that class run inside the container, then Payara Micro stops — giving each class a clean container environment
B) Payara Micro starts exactly once at the very beginning of the test run, stays continuously running across all test classes, and only shuts down at the absolute end
C) All @Test methods execute entirely outside the container on the local JVM; only the @Deployment archive itself is physically deployed to Payara Micro for resolution
D) Payara Micro is launched solely to execute the static @Deployment factory method; the actual @Test methods themselves never run inside the container context
questions.explanation: In the embedded Arquillian + Payara Micro setup, the lifecycle is per test class: start container → deploy → run all @Test methods → stop. This ensures test isolation but adds startup overhead per class.

questions.question: Eclipse MicroProfile is an 'umbrella specification' that:
options: A) Defines only monitoring and observability APIs specifically targeting application server infrastructure management
B) Is a commercial product jointly developed, sold, and supported by IBM and Red Hat under subscription licensing
C) Is intended to completely replace all existing Jakarta EE specifications with smaller, lighter cloud-oriented alternatives
D) Gathers a focused set of APIs for cloud-native microservices, from the Eclipse Foundation
questions.explanation: MicroProfile (started September 2016) is an open-source Eclipse project providing cloud-native APIs: Config, Health, Metrics, Fault Tolerance, JWT, Rest Client, and more.

questions.question: MicroProfile Config's three mandatory configuration sources are:
options: A) System properties, environment variables, and META-INF/microprofile-config.properties
B) Live configuration from database tables, an LDAP directory server, and remote REST endpoints as dynamic sources
C) Deployment descriptor files web.xml, beans.xml, and persistence.xml that contain static runtime configuration
D) YAML files, JSON configuration files, and .env dotfiles loaded from the application's working directory path
questions.explanation: Every MicroProfile Config implementation must support: system properties (priority 400), environment variables (priority 300), and microprofile-config.properties (priority 100).

questions.question: MicroProfile Health's three health check types are:
options: A) Readiness, Liveness, Startup
B) Active probe, Passive probe, and Scheduled probe — three timing-strategy health check execution categories
C) CPU utilisation check, Memory usage check, and Disk space check — three system resource health metrics
D) HTTP ping check, JMX attribute monitor, and Log error rate sensor — three protocol-level health probe types
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Health defines: @Liveness (is the app alive?), @Readiness (is it ready to serve traffic?), and @Startup (has it finished initialising?).

questions.question: MicroProfile OpenAPI exposes generated documentation at which HTTP endpoint?
options: A) /swagger
B) /openapi
C) /api-docs
D) /wadl — the Web Application Description Language endpoint for describing RESTful service metadata in XML
questions.explanation: MicroProfile OpenAPI automatically generates OpenAPI v3 documents and exposes them at the /openapi endpoint.

questions.question: MicroProfile Fault Tolerance provides which resilience annotations?
options: A) @Transactional, @Async, and @Schedule — container-managed EJB annotations shared with Jakarta EE services
B) @Cached, @Throttled, and @RateLimit — proprietary rate-limiting and caching annotations for REST resources
C) @Retry, @Fallback, @Timeout, @CircuitBreaker (and others)
D) @Resilient, @Bulkhead, and @SafeCall — non-standard annotations from a third-party resilience library
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Fault Tolerance defines @Retry, @Fallback, @Timeout, @CircuitBreaker, and @Bulkhead for resilient method-level execution.

questions.question: MicroProfile JWT RBAC secures endpoints using:
options: A) Cryptographically signed JWTs carrying user identity and group claims
B) Session cookies paired with CSRF tokens stored server-side to maintain authenticated state across page requests
C) SOAP WS-Security headers carrying X.509 digital certificates used for mutual TLS authentication on every call
D) HTTP Basic authentication with bcrypt-hashed passwords verified by the IdentityStore on every incoming request
questions.explanation: MicroProfile JWT RBAC authenticates via a signed JWT containing the user's subject and groups. @RolesAllowed checks those groups for access control.

questions.question: MicroProfile Rest Client allows you to call REST services by:
options: A) Using a generated SOAP client stub derived from the remote service's WSDL contract definition under the hood
B) Writing raw HTTP socket code using Java's URLConnection API and manually encoding the JSON request payloads
C) Defining a JAX-RS-annotated interface — the framework generates the HTTP client implementation
D) Manually constructing JSON request payloads using JSON-P JsonObjectBuilder and dispatching them via JAX-RS client
questions.explanation: Annotate an interface with @RegisterRestClient and JAX-RS annotations. MicroProfile generates an HTTP proxy you inject and call like a local bean.

questions.question: MicroProfile OpenTelemetry sends distributed traces to backends such as:
options: A) Only Grafana and Kibana — no other telemetry backend exporters are supported by the OpenTelemetry spec
B) Splunk and Datadog exclusively
C) Jaeger, Zipkin, or Instana
D) Prometheus and InfluxDB only
questions.explanation: MicroProfile OpenTelemetry integrates with the CNCF OpenTelemetry project. Traces can be exported to Jaeger, Zipkin, Instana, and other compatible backends.

questions.question: The key distinction between Jakarta EE and MicroProfile application styles is:
options: A) Jakarta EE targets monolithic/web enterprise apps while MicroProfile targets cloud-native microservices
B) Jakarta EE is open-source community-governed while MicroProfile is a commercial IBM product requiring a subscription
C) Jakarta EE is restricted to SOAP-based web services only while MicroProfile exclusively supports RESTful HTTP services
D) MicroProfile is intended to completely supersede all Jakarta EE specifications and serve as its full replacement
questions.explanation: Jakarta EE covers a broad enterprise scope including monolithic .war/.ear deployments, while MicroProfile specifically targets cloud-native microservices. They complement each other.

questions.question: MicroProfile Metrics exposes collected metrics over HTTP in which format(s)?
options: A) JSON or Prometheus format
B) CSV comma-separated format only — consuming tools must parse the flat file structure themselves
C) XML document format only — metrics are exposed as structured XML conforming to a fixed schema definition
D) Binary Protocol Buffers format only — metrics are encoded in compact Protobuf for efficiency and low overhead
questions.explanation: MicroProfile Metrics exposes metrics via REST in either JSON or Prometheus text format, consumable by Prometheus scrapers and tools like Grafana.

questions.question: Jakarta EE and MicroProfile share which values?
options: A) Proprietary licensing models with single-vendor governance requiring paid commercial support subscriptions
B) They share absolutely no values and are designed as mutually exclusive, directly competing standards
C) Standardisation, openness, stability, ease of development, and portability
D) Mandatory Docker container packaging as the only officially supported deployment and runtime execution model
questions.explanation: Both Jakarta EE and Eclipse MicroProfile share the values of standardisation, openness, stability, ease of development, and portability.

questions.question: To read a MicroProfile Config value programmatically (not via injection), you use:
options: A) System.getenv('my.property') — reading the value directly from the operating system environment variables
B) Config config = ConfigProvider.getConfig(); config.getValue('my.property', String.class)
C) new Properties().load(stream).getProperty('my.property') — loading a .properties file via the ClassLoader API
D) @ConfigProperty(name='my.property') on a field — which is the declarative @Inject-based injection alternative
questions.explanation: ConfigProvider.getConfig() retrieves the runtime Config instance; getValue() fetches a typed value. @ConfigProperty is the declarative injection alternative.

options: A) @RegisterRestClient generates an automatically created mock stub of SwapiService for use in unit testing scenarios only
B) @RegisterRestClient converts SwapiService into a JMS message producer; @Inject @RestClient injects the queue destination
C) The annotated type must be a concrete class — plain Java interfaces cannot be registered as MicroProfile Rest Client types

D) @RegisterRestClient marks the interface so MicroProfile generates a working HTTP client implementation; @Inject @RestClient injects that generated implementation — calling swapiService.getCharacter('1') makes a real GET HTTP call to the configured URL
questions.explanation: @RegisterRestClient tells MicroProfile Rest Client to generate a proxy implementation for this interface. The base URL is configured in microprofile-config.properties. @Inject @RestClient injects the proxy — method calls on it become real HTTP requests.

options: A) @Retry spawns the method concurrently on multiple parallel threads; @Timeout kills the slowest thread; @Fallback merges the surviving results together
B) All three annotations are fully interchangeable aliases that represent the exact same resilience behaviour internally
C) @Timeout aborts the call if it takes too long; @Retry automatically retries the call on failure; @Fallback calls calculateAmountFallback() if all retries are exhausted or a timeout occurs
D) @Timeout records method execution timing to a metrics sink; @Retry logs each individual retry attempt; @Fallback logs the root failure cause
questions.explanation: @Timeout sets a maximum execution time and aborts if exceeded. @Retry retries the method invocation on failure. @Fallback specifies a fallback method (calculateAmountFallback) to call when all retry attempts fail or a non-retryable error occurs — combining three resilience patterns on a single method.

options: A) @Liveness designates this as a startup probe that runs only once when the application first completes initialisation
B) The /health endpoint returns HTTP 503 with status 'DOWN' and the remaining disk space in MB, because status(false) marks the check as DOWN
C) The division by 1024 throws an ArithmeticException when freeSpace is exactly zero due to integer division rules
D) The /health endpoint always returns HTTP 200 with status 'UP' because the HealthCheck interface defaults to UP always
questions.explanation: .status(freeSpace > 100) sets status to UP if freeSpace > 100, DOWN otherwise. When DOWN, the /health endpoint returns HTTP 503 Service Unavailable with a JSON body showing status:'DOWN' and the remainingSpace data. @Liveness marks it as a liveness probe (is the app alive?).
